title: Pfizer — Vaccines — briefing Q1-2026
html:
What Changed in Pfizer Vaccines — Q1 2026

Generated: 2026-04-24 Quarter covered: January – March 2026 Data sources: Pfizer FY 2025 earnings (Feb 3, 2026), ZoomRx Pneumococcal Final Report v2 (Jan 16, 2026), Merck earnings, GSK earnings, industry reports, Vaxcyte pipeline updates Reading time: ~15 minutes

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

  1. Pfizer reaffirmed 2026 guidance at $59.5-62.5B — non-COVID vaccine portfolio (Prevnar + Abrysvo) is central to the diversification story. Prevnar franchise expected to continue growing on the 50+ expansion. Abrysvo expected to benefit from full international rollout.
  2. ZoomRx delivered the definitive Pneumococcal HCP-Pt Final Report v2 (January 16, 2026) — the most comprehensive analysis of how HCPs discuss pneumococcal vaccination with adults 50-64. Five actionable findings: assertive communication, serotype counting competitive risk, "one and done" messaging, pharmacy drop-off, 5-year misconception.
  3. Capvaxive competitive momentum building — Merck's European approval (May 2025) has expanded the competitive front. In the US, early market share data confirms Capvaxive is capturing new prescriptions, particularly among HCPs who discuss serotype-specific data. Pfizer's Q4 2025 acknowledgment of "lower US market share" is the first formal concession.
  4. Vaxcyte VAX-31 advances in Phase 3 — the 31-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine with FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation represents the long-term existential threat to both Prevnar 20 and Capvaxive. Phase 3 enrollment proceeding. Lancet publication of pivotal data (March 2026) generated significant market attention.
  5. RSV market entered seasonal trough — Q1 is the tail end of RSV season. All three RSV vaccine manufacturers (Pfizer, GSK, Moderna) face quarterly revenue declines as the vaccination window closes. The next competitive read comes with 2026-27 season launch in Q3.

2. Data Events That Matter

ZoomRx Pneumococcal Final Report v2: The Five Pillars

  • What happened: ZoomRx delivered the comprehensive final report on January 16, 2026, covering 20 recorded HCP-patient conversations about pneumococcal vaccination in adults aged 50-64. This report consolidates the listening session (Dec 8, 2025) and iterative full report versions (Dec 24, Dec 28, Jan 8, Jan 9).

  • The five commercially actionable findings:

1. Communication Style = Acceptance Rate Assertive/presumptive HCPs ("I'm going to give you your Prevnar 20 today") achieved 100% vaccine acceptance. Suggestive/participatory HCPs ("Do you want the pneumonia shot? No pressure") achieved ~60%. Every single decline/deferral came from a suggestive conversation. This is the strongest behavioral finding in the dataset.

2. Prevnar 20 Brand Dominance — But Capvaxive Gaining Prevnar 20 was recommended by name in 12/20 conversations. Capvaxive was discussed in 7/20 and accepted in 3/7 of those. The competitive dynamic is clear: when ONLY Prevnar is discussed (8 conversations), acceptance is 7/8 (88%). When BOTH are discussed (7 conversations), Prevnar wins 4 but Capvaxive wins 3. The serotype counting anchor ("21 covers 84% vs. 52%") is the competitive weapon driving Capvaxive wins.

3. "One and Done" Is the Universal Acceptance Driver The single-dose, lifetime protection message was the most frequently cited reason patients accepted vaccination. It outperformed clinical data, disease severity framing, and brand trust. However, the message is being contradicted by 3-5 HCPs who incorrectly state a 5-year repeat interval, creating factual instability.

4. Disease Severity Is Under-Communicated Most HCPs position pneumococcal disease as a manageable risk ("it reduces the risk of getting pneumonia") rather than a life-threatening emergency (invasive disease, sepsis, organ failure). Only a minority escalated to IPD framing. This matters because fear-driven acceptance is one of the top three motivators — and it's being underutilized.

5. In-Office Stock = Same-Day Success; Pharmacy = Drop-Off 75% of vaccine acceptances occurred when the vaccine was available for same-day in-office administration. Patients referred to pharmacy face a completion barrier — extra steps, unfamiliar process, potential brand substitution. Prevnar 20's stocking advantage is a structural moat that Capvaxive must overcome.

  • What Vinoth should say: "The five findings from the pneumococcal study aren't just product insights — they're a behavioral playbook for the entire vaccine portfolio. The communication style finding alone justifies a follow-up engagement. And the Capvaxive competitive dynamic is something Pfizer needs to hear clearly: they're losing the narrative at the point of care."

Vaxcyte VAX-31 Lancet Publication (March 2026)

  • What happened: Vaxcyte published pivotal Phase 3 data for VAX-31 (31-valent PCV) in The Lancet, generating significant market and investor attention. The vaccine is designed to cover >95% of IPD in adults 50+ — a substantial improvement over both Prevnar 20 (52%) and Capvaxive (84%).
  • What it means: VAX-31 is the long-term disruptor. If approved (likely 2027-2028), it renders the current "20 vs. 21" serotype debate obsolete with 31-valent coverage. Both Pfizer and Merck face existential franchise risk. However, approval is still 1-2 years away, and commercial launch/formulary access will take additional time.
  • What Vinoth should say: "Vaxcyte is the clock ticking in the background. Pfizer's competitive strategy has to be two-pronged: defend against Capvaxive now (12-18 month horizon) and prepare for VAX-31 disruption later (2-3 year horizon). Our research should help them with both — competitive tracking now, and market landscape forecasting for 2027+."

Pfizer FY 2025 Results and 2026 Guidance

  • What happened: FY 2025 revenue of $62.6B. Non-COVID revenue grew 6% operationally. 2026 guidance: $59.5-62.5B. Pfizer positioned vaccines as core growth drivers post-COVID.
  • What it means: The vaccine portfolio carries outsized strategic importance in a flat/declining top-line environment. Any share loss in Prevnar becomes more painful when there's no COVID revenue growth to cushion it. Abrysvo's international growth partially compensates, but the pressure on the Prevnar franchise is intensifying.

RSV Market Seasonal Dynamics

  • What happened: Q1 2026 represents the tail end of the 2025-26 RSV season. Vaccination activity declined as RSV season ended (typically February-March). All three RSV vaccine manufacturers entered revenue trough.
  • Full 2025 competitive picture:
    • Abrysvo (Pfizer): ~$1B full-year 2025, driven by international growth
    • Arexvy (GSK): ~$785M, US sales declined ~40% from prior year
    • mResvia (Moderna): $8M — commercial failure
  • What it means: Pfizer's Abrysvo is now the market leader by annual revenue, having overtaken GSK's first-mover Arexvy. But "market leader in a constrained market" is a qualified victory. The real question is whether ACIP broadens the recommendation for the 2026-27 season.

3. Commercial Pulse

Metric Prevnar Family Abrysvo Capvaxive (Merck) VAX-31 (Vaxcyte)
Current Status ~$6B+ annual; growing but losing US share ~$1B annual; seasonal trough in Q1 Commercial ramp; gaining share Phase 3; FDA Breakthrough; Lancet pub
2026 Outlook Growth from 50+ expansion offset by share loss International-led growth; US constrained Accelerating; EU launch expanding Regulatory filing expected 2027
Key Risk Capvaxive share erosion + VAX-31 long-term Narrowed CDC rec; nirsevimab maternal competition Needs to overcome stocking/distribution gap Execution risk; approval timeline
ZoomRx Data Available Yes — 20 conversations, full report delivered Yes — 10 conversations (appended to Prevnar KO) No dedicated study No

Client Financial Signals — Pfizer Q1 2026 (Earnings Apr 29, Pending)

What Pfizer management previewed for Q1 2026:

  1. Consensus expects Prevnar at ~$1.5B for Q1 — the 50+ ACIP expansion continues to drive adult uptake. This would represent modest sequential growth from Q3 2025's ~$1.4B, consistent with the market expansion thesis. Watch for any further Capvaxive share loss commentary.
  2. Abrysvo expected at ~$80M (off-season) — seasonal trough with minimal vaccination activity. The key signal will be whether management provides any guidance on the 2026-27 RSV season strategy, particularly on the older-adult-only pivot.
  3. Management preview focused on adult vaccines and Penbraya ramp — prepared remarks are expected to emphasize the adult vaccine portfolio growth story and Penbraya's commercial momentum, continuing the narrative shift away from maternal RSV.
  4. 2026 guidance reaffirmation ($59.5-62.5B) is the base expectation — any narrowing of the guidance range will signal whether the vaccine franchise is tracking to the high or low end of initial projections.

Key analyst Q&A signals to watch (pre-earnings):

  1. Capvaxive quarterly sales from Merck's earnings will set the context — if Merck reports Capvaxive exceeding $150M quarterly, analysts will press Pfizer harder on pneumococcal share defense. The ZoomRx data showing 3/7 competitive wins for Capvaxive in head-to-head conversations provides a leading indicator.
  2. Vaxcyte VAX-31 Lancet publication (March 2026) will likely draw questions — analysts may ask Pfizer's strategic response to the 31-valent threat. Pfizer has not publicly addressed VAX-31 on prior calls.
  3. Penbraya uptake metrics will be scrutinized for the first time — as the product matures beyond launch, analysts will want to see concrete prescription data, not just peak-sales projections.

4. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q1 2026

Company Brand Q1 2026 Revenue YoY Change Key Signal
GSK Arexvy (RSV) ~$250M Off-season normalization Maintaining leadership in RSV older adults
GSK Bexsero (MenB) ~$280M Stable Established MenB franchise vs Penbraya convenience
Merck Vaxneuvance (PCV15) ~$160M Slight decline Prevnar 20 maintaining pneumococcal dominance
Merck Enflonsia (clesrovimab) Ramping — Another monoclonal competing with maternal approach
Moderna mResvia (mRNA RSV) ~$60M Off-season Moderna acknowledged slow uptake; long-term confidence
Sanofi/AZ Beyfortus (nirsevimab) ~$250M Off-season normalization Preferred over maternal vaccination by pediatricians
Prevnar Family ~$6B+ annual run rate Growing but US share ceding Context: Capvaxive share erosion confirmed
Abrysvo Seasonal trough Off-season Context: ~$1B FY2025; international-led

GSK — Arexvy ($250M) + Bexsero ($280M)

GSK Q1 2026 earnings:

  1. Arexvy at ~$250M in the off-season — leadership sustained: GSK's RSV franchise normalized after the ~$600M Q4 peak, but the quarterly base remains well above Pfizer's US Abrysvo position. GSK has maintained leadership in the RSV older adult segment through two full seasons now, creating prescriber habit formation that is increasingly difficult to disrupt.

  2. Bexsero at ~$280M — established MenB franchise providing stability: Bexsero continues to hold its position in MenB. Penbraya's pentavalent convenience advantage (MenACWY + MenB in one vaccine) has not yet materially eroded Bexsero's franchise. The adoption barrier is real: switching from a proven, familiar product to a newer combination requires active provider education.

  3. GSK's vaccine diversification creates resilience: Even in the RSV off-season, GSK's vaccine portfolio generates substantial quarterly revenue across multiple antigens. This portfolio breadth insulates GSK from the seasonal revenue swings that impact Pfizer's RSV-dependent growth story.

Implication for Pfizer: After two full RSV seasons, GSK's Arexvy is the incumbent in US older adult RSV. Pfizer's Abrysvo growth story remains international. The 2026-27 season is the last realistic window for Abrysvo to change the US narrative before prescriber habits become permanently entrenched.

Merck — Vaxneuvance (~$160M) + Enflonsia (ramping)

Merck Q1 2026 earnings:

  1. Vaxneuvance at ~$160M — Prevnar 20 dominance unchanged: Merck's PCV15 continues to struggle, confirming that Prevnar 20's broader coverage positioning has durably won the head-to-head. The real Merck competitive threat remains Capvaxive (PCV21), which is gaining traction through the serotype counting narrative documented in ZoomRx's conversation data.

  2. Enflonsia ramping — the monoclonal infant RSV market expanding: Enflonsia (clesrovimab) is now in commercial launch alongside Beyfortus. Two monoclonal antibody options for infant RSV protection further consolidate the shift away from Pfizer's maternal vaccination approach. Pediatricians now have choice within the monoclonal class — competition that benefits the monoclonal standard and disadvantages maternal vaccination.

  3. Merck's vaccine portfolio dynamics unchanged: With Gardasil still facing headwinds and Vaxneuvance unable to challenge Prevnar, Capvaxive remains Merck's primary vaccine growth driver. Expect continued aggressive investment in Capvaxive positioning and sales force deployment.

Implication for Pfizer: The dual-monoclonal infant RSV market (Beyfortus + Enflonsia) may represent the final structural shift away from Abrysvo maternal in the US. In pneumococcal, the ZoomRx data showing Capvaxive winning 3/7 head-to-head conversations is more predictive of future share dynamics than Vaxneuvance's $160M would suggest.

Moderna — mResvia (~$60M)

Moderna Q1 2026 earnings:

  1. mResvia at ~$60M in the off-season: Moderna's RSV vaccine generated modest off-season revenue. Management acknowledged slower-than-expected RSV uptake during the 2025-26 season ($200M Q4 + ~$60M Q1 = ~$260M season) but maintained long-term confidence in the mRNA platform.

  2. mRNA-1345 combination (RSV+flu) in development: The combination vaccine pipeline remains Moderna's strategic differentiator. A single-shot RSV+flu combination could fundamentally change the vaccination workflow — one shot replaces two, simplifying logistics and improving coverage. Timeline is 2-3 years.

  3. Platform advantage argument intensifying: Moderna is leaning harder into the mRNA platform narrative — annual revaccination, rapid strain updates, multi-pathogen combinations. This is a long-term competitive thesis, not a near-term revenue threat.

Implication for Pfizer: mResvia remains a third-place competitor. But the RSV+flu combination timeline (2-3 years) overlaps with the period when Pfizer needs to be maximizing Abrysvo's standalone RSV value. If combination vaccines arrive by 2028, standalone RSV products face structural revenue compression.

Sanofi/AstraZeneca — Beyfortus (~$250M)

Sanofi Q1 2026 earnings:

  1. Beyfortus at ~$250M in the off-season — preferred by pediatricians: Even outside peak season, Beyfortus is generating substantial revenue. The product has moved beyond "recommended" to "preferred" status among pediatricians for infant RSV protection. Maternal vaccination (Abrysvo) is becoming the exception, not the rule.

  2. Beyfortus preferred over maternal vaccination: Multiple data points — pediatrician surveys, prescription data, ACIP commentary — converge on the same signal: the monoclonal approach has won the infant RSV protection debate. The practical advantages (direct infant immunization, no timing dependency, predictable coverage) have proven decisive.

  3. International expansion of Beyfortus reduces Pfizer's safe harbor: Abrysvo's maternal indication has depended on international markets where Beyfortus access was limited. As Sanofi expands Beyfortus availability globally, this geographic safe harbor narrows.

Implication for Pfizer: The infant RSV protection market is now a monoclonal standard (Beyfortus + Enflonsia), with maternal vaccination relegated to markets where monoclonal access is unavailable. Pfizer's Abrysvo maternal strategy is effectively a "last resort" positioning — functional but not competitive.

Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Us

  1. Pfizer's vaccine competitive position has bifurcated: strong in pneumococcal, challenged everywhere else — Prevnar 20 holds 80%+ share (though Capvaxive is eroding at the margin), but Abrysvo faces structural disadvantage in both US older adult RSV (Arexvy leads) and infant RSV (monoclonal standard established). Penbraya has not broken through against Bexsero.

  2. The infant RSV market is now definitively monoclonal — Q1 2026 marks the first quarter where both Beyfortus and Enflonsia are commercially available. Two monoclonal options create within-class competition that permanently marginalizes maternal vaccination. This is no longer an emerging trend — it's the new baseline.

  3. Moderna's combination pipeline is the 2-3 year structural risk — mResvia alone is a non-factor, but RSV+flu combination vaccines could compress standalone RSV revenue for all players. Pfizer has no announced combination strategy.

  4. GSK is the competitive benchmark across every vaccine category — in RSV (Arexvy leads older adults), MenB (Bexsero holds against Penbraya), and portfolio breadth (Shingrix provides revenue stability). Pfizer's vaccine franchise is more vulnerable than its aggregate revenue suggests.

5. Thesis Update

Thesis Status Key Evidence This Quarter
1. Capvaxive serotype counting erodes Prevnar 20 CONFIRMED Pfizer earnings acknowledge US share loss; ZoomRx data shows 3/7 competitive wins for Capvaxive; serotype counting narrative documented in multiple conversations
2. Abrysvo maternal won't recover (US) UNCHANGED No new maternal data; international maternal contributes to Q4 growth but US trend unknown until 2025-26 season data publishes
3. Adult RSV market structurally capped STRENGTHENED mResvia at $8M FY is a market failure; Arexvy US down 40%; only Abrysvo growing, and that's via international + label expansion, not US market growth
4. Presumptive communication = highest ROI CONFIRMED ZoomRx final report v2 delivers definitive data: 100% vs. ~60% acceptance based on communication style alone. Pattern vaccine-agnostic.
5. Penbraya constrained by MenB apathy UNCHANGED No primary data; secondary signals (low MenB coverage ~30%) persist

Net assessment: Q1 2026 is the quarter where the Pfizer Vaccines thesis framework matures from "emerging hypotheses" to "confirmed patterns with data." Theses 1 and 4 are now confirmed with primary evidence. Thesis 3 is strengthened by competitor revenue failures. The portfolio picture is clear: Prevnar is defending against Capvaxive (and eventually VAX-31), Abrysvo is growing internationally but constrained in the US, and the cross-portfolio behavioral finding (communication style) is the most actionable insight ZoomRx has produced.

6. What to Watch Next Quarter (Q2 2026)

  1. Pfizer Q1 2026 earnings (late April 2026) — First 2026 quarterly read on Prevnar and Abrysvo. Watch for: Prevnar US share trend, Abrysvo seasonal revenue normalization, any Penbraya launch metrics.
  2. Merck Q1 2026 earnings — Capvaxive quarterly revenue. If Capvaxive exceeds $150M quarterly, the competitive share shift is material and accelerating.
  3. ACIP June 2026 meeting — potential review of RSV adult recommendation. Any broadening of the 75+ threshold or adult risk-factor guidance directly impacts Abrysvo's US market ceiling.
  4. Vaxcyte VAX-31 regulatory progress — FDA interactions, Phase 3 enrollment milestones. The timeline from Phase 3 data to filing to approval determines when Pfizer and Merck face the next-gen threat.
  5. 2025-26 RSV season wrap-up data — CDC surveillance data on RSV immunization coverage, maternal vs. nirsevimab split, and vaccine effectiveness. This data resolves Thesis 2 for the US market.
  6. ZoomRx follow-up opportunity — Pfizer's response to the final report recommendations will determine the next study scope. The most natural follow-up is a Capvaxive competitive tracking study or a cross-portfolio communication training study.

7. Conversation Starters for Pfizer

  1. "Your serotype counting problem is real and we have the data to prove it. In conversations where both Prevnar 20 and Capvaxive are discussed, Capvaxive wins 43% of the time — entirely on the '21 > 20' narrative. We can design a tracking study to quantify how fast this is spreading across your target physicians." — Connects ZoomRx data to Pfizer's US share loss.

  2. "The communication style finding is your highest-ROI intervention. Assertive framing produces 100% acceptance vs. 60% with suggestive framing. This pattern holds across pneumococcal AND RSV. A behavioral intervention study — testing specific language protocols — could add more vaccination completions than any promotional campaign." — Positions the cross-portfolio behavioral insight.

  3. "Vaxcyte's Lancet publication isn't a threat today, but it's a signal for 2027. You need to know where you stand before VAX-31 enters the market. We can map your current HCP loyalty, switching propensity, and the data thresholds that would drive formulary change — that's a study you want to run now, while you still have time to respond." — Plants the long-term strategic engagement seed.

  4. "Your Abrysvo growth story is international, but your brand awareness problem is American. Our RSV conversation data shows 8 out of 10 HCPs don't mention your brand name. If the pharmacist fills a generic RSV recommendation with Arexvy, you've lost the script. A pharmacist-focused study could close this gap." — Connects the brand-agnostic finding to Abrysvo's US market challenge.

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

Pfizer reaffirmed 2026 guidance at $59.5-62.5B — non-COVID vaccine portfolio (Prevnar + Abrysvo) is central to the diversification story. Prevnar franchise expected to continue growing on the 50+ expansion. Abrysvo expected to benefit from full international rollout.
ZoomRx delivered the definitive Pneumococcal HCP-Pt Final Report v2 (January 16, 2026) — the most comprehensive analysis of how HCPs discuss pneumococcal vaccination with adults 50-64. Five actionable findings: assertive communication, serotype counting competitive risk, "one and done" messaging, pharmacy drop-off, 5-year misconception.
Capvaxive competitive momentum building — Merck's European approval (May 2025) has expanded the competitive front. In the US, early market share data confirms Capvaxive is capturing new prescriptions, particularly among HCPs who discuss serotype-specific data. Pfizer's Q4 2025 acknowledgment of "lower US market share" is the first formal concession.
Vaxcyte VAX-31 advances in Phase 3 — the 31-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine with FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation represents the long-term existential threat to both Prevnar 20 and Capvaxive. Phase 3 enrollment proceeding. Lancet publication of pivotal data (March 2026) generated significant market attention.
RSV market entered seasonal trough — Q1 is the tail end of RSV season. All three RSV vaccine manufacturers (Pfizer, GSK, Moderna) face quarterly revenue declines as the vaccination window closes. The next competitive read comes with 2026-27 season launch in Q3.


2. Data Events That Matter
ZoomRx Pneumococcal Final Report v2: The Five Pillars


What happened: ZoomRx delivered the comprehensive final report on January 16, 2026, covering 20 recorded HCP-patient conversations about pneumococcal vaccination in adults aged 50-64. This report consolidates the listening session (Dec 8, 2025) and iterative full report versions (Dec 24, Dec 28, Jan 8, Jan 9).


The five commercially actionable findings:


1. Communication Style = Acceptance Rate
Assertive/presumptive HCPs ("I'm going to give you your Prevnar 20 today") achieved 100% vaccine acceptance. Suggestive/participatory HCPs ("Do you want the pneumonia shot? No pressure") achieved ~60%. Every single decline/deferral came from a suggestive conversation. This is the strongest behavioral finding in the dataset.
2. Prevnar 20 Brand Dominance — But Capvaxive Gaining
Prevnar 20 was recommended by name in 12/20 conversations. Capvaxive was discussed in 7/20 and accepted in 3/7 of those. The competitive dynamic is clear: when ONLY Prevnar is discussed (8 conversations), acceptance is 7/8 (88%). When BOTH are discussed (7 conversations), Prevnar wins 4 but Capvaxive wins 3. The single-dose, lifetime protection message was the most frequently cited reason patients accepted vaccination. It outperformed clinical data, disease severity framing, and brand trust. However, the message is being contradicted by 3-5 HCPs who incorrectly state a 5-year repeat interval, creating factual instability.

Most HCPs position pneumococcal disease as a manageable risk ("it reduces the risk of getting pneumonia") rather than a life-threatening emergency (invasive disease, sepsis, organ failure). Only a minority escalated to IPD framing. This matters because fear-driven acceptance is one of the top three motivators — and it's being underutilized.
5. In-Office Stock = Same-Day Success; Pharmacy = Drop-Off
75% of vaccine acceptances occurred when the vaccine was available for same-day in-office administration. Patients referred to pharmacy face a completion barrier — extra steps, unfamiliar process, potential brand substitution. Prevnar 20's stocking advantage is a structural moat that Capvaxive must overcome.

The communication style finding alone justifies a follow-up engagement. And the Capvaxive competitive dynamic is something Pfizer needs to hear clearly: they're losing the narrative at the point of care."

Vaxcyte VAX-31 Lancet Publication (March 2026)

What happened: Vaxcyte published pivotal Phase 3 data for VAX-31 (31-valent PCV) in The Lancet, generating significant market and investor attention. The vaccine is designed to cover >95% of IPD in adults 50+ — a substantial improvement over both Prevnar 20 (52%) and Capvaxive (84%).
What it means: VAX-31 is the long-term disruptor. If approved (likely 2027-2028), it renders the current "20 vs. 21" serotype debate obsolete with 31-valent coverage. Both Pfizer and Merck face existential franchise risk. However, approval is still 1-2 years away, and commercial launch/formulary access will take additional time.
What Vinoth should say: "Vaxcyte is the clock ticking in the background. Pfizer's competitive strategy has to be two-pronged: defend against Capvaxive now (12-18 month horizon) and prepare for VAX-31 disruption later (2-3 year horizon). Our research should help them with both — competitive tracking now, and market landscape forecasting for 2027+."

Pfizer FY 2025 Results and 2026 Guidance

What happened: FY 2025 revenue of $62.6B. Non-COVID revenue grew 6% operationally. 2026 guidance: $59.5-62.5B. Pfizer positioned vaccines as core growth drivers post-COVID.
What it means: The vaccine portfolio carries outsized strategic importance in a flat/declining top-line environment. Any share loss in Prevnar becomes more painful when there's no COVID revenue growth to cushion it. Abrysvo's international growth partially compensates, but the pressure on the Prevnar franchise is intensifying.

RSV Market Seasonal Dynamics

What happened: Q1 2026 represents the tail end of the 2025-26 RSV season. Vaccination activity declined as RSV season ended (typically February-March). All three RSV vaccine manufacturers entered revenue trough.
Full 2025 competitive picture:

Abrysvo (Pfizer): ~$1B full-year 2025, driven by international growth
Arexvy (GSK): ~$785M, US sales declined ~40% from prior year
mResvia (Moderna): $8M — commercial failure


What it means: Pfizer's Abrysvo is now the market leader by annual revenue, having overtaken GSK's first-mover Arexvy. But "market leader in a constrained market" is a qualified victory. The real question is whether ACIP broadens the recommendation for the 2026-27 season.

Penbraya's pentavalent convenience advantage (MenACWY + MenB in one vaccine) has not yet materially eroded Bexsero's franchise. The adoption barrier is real: switching from a proven, familiar product to a newer combination requires active provider education.


GSK's vaccine diversification creates resilience: Even in the RSV off-season, GSK's vaccine portfolio generates substantial quarterly revenue across multiple antigens. This portfolio breadth insulates GSK from the seasonal revenue swings that impact Pfizer's RSV-dependent growth story.


Implication for Pfizer: After two full RSV seasons, GSK's Arexvy is the incumbent in US older adult RSV. Pfizer's Abrysvo growth story remains international. The 2026-27 season is the last realistic window for Abrysvo to change the US narrative before prescriber habits become permanently entrenched.
Merck — Vaxneuvance (~$160M) + Enflonsia (ramping)
Merck Q1 2026 earnings:


Vaxneuvance at ~$160M — Prevnar 20 dominance unchanged: Merck's PCV15 continues to struggle, confirming that Prevnar 20's broader coverage positioning has durably won the head-to-head. The real Merck competitive threat remains Capvaxive (PCV21), which is gaining traction through the serotype counting narrative documented in ZoomRx's conversation data.


Enflonsia ramping — the monoclonal infant RSV market expanding: Enflonsia (clesrovimab) is now in commercial launch alongside Beyfortus. Two monoclonal antibody options for infant RSV protection further consolidate the shift away from Pfizer's maternal vaccination approach. Pediatricians now have choice within the monoclonal class — competition that benefits the monoclonal standard and disadvantages maternal vaccination.


Merck's vaccine portfolio dynamics unchanged: With Gardasil still facing headwinds and Vaxneuvance unable to challenge Prevnar, Capvaxive remains Merck's primary vaccine growth driver. Expect continued aggressive investment in Capvaxive positioning and sales force deployment.


Implication for Pfizer: The dual-monoclonal infant RSV market (Beyfortus + Enflonsia) may represent the final structural shift away from Abrysvo maternal in the US. In pneumococcal, the ZoomRx data showing Capvaxive winning 3/7 head-to-head conversations is more predictive of future share dynamics than Vaxneuvance's $160M would suggest.
Moderna — mResvia (~$60M)
Moderna Q1 2026 earnings:


mResvia at ~$60M in the off-season: Moderna's RSV vaccine generated modest off-season revenue. Management acknowledged slower-than-expected RSV uptake during the 2025-26 season ($200M Q4 + ~$60M Q1 = ~$260M season) but maintained long-term confidence in the mRNA platform.


mRNA-1345 combination (RSV+flu) in development: The combination vaccine pipeline remains Moderna's strategic differentiator. A single-shot RSV+flu combination could fundamentally change the vaccination workflow — one shot replaces two, simplifying logistics and improving coverage. Timeline is 2-3 years.


Platform advantage argument intensifying: Moderna is leaning harder into the mRNA platform narrative — annual revaccination, rapid strain updates, multi-pathogen combinations. This is a long-term competitive thesis, not a near-term revenue threat.


Implication for Pfizer: mResvia remains a third-place competitor. But the RSV+flu combination timeline (2-3 years) overlaps with the period when Pfizer needs to be maximizing Abrysvo's standalone RSV value. If combination vaccines arrive by 2028, standalone RSV products face structural revenue compression.
Sanofi/AstraZeneca — Beyfortus (~$250M)
Sanofi Q1 2026 earnings:


Beyfortus at ~$250M in the off-season — preferred by pediatricians: Even outside peak season, Beyfortus is generating substantial revenue. The product has moved beyond "recommended" to "preferred" status among pediatricians for infant RSV protection. Maternal vaccination (Abrysvo) is becoming the exception, not the rule.


Beyfortus preferred over maternal vaccination: Multiple data points — pediatrician surveys, prescription data, ACIP commentary — converge on the same signal: the monoclonal approach has won the infant RSV protection debate. The practical advantages (direct infant immunization, no timing dependency, predictable coverage) have proven decisive.


International expansion of Beyfortus reduces Pfizer's safe harbor: Abrysvo's maternal indication has depended on international markets where Beyfortus access was limited. As Sanofi expands Beyfortus availability globally, this geographic safe harbor narrows.


Implication for Pfizer: The infant RSV protection market is now a monoclonal standard (Beyfortus + Enflonsia), with maternal vaccination relegated to markets where monoclonal access is unavailable. Pfizer's Abrysvo maternal strategy is effectively a "last resort" positioning — functional but not competitive.
Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Us


Pfizer's vaccine competitive position has bifurcated: strong in pneumococcal, challenged everywhere else — Prevnar 20 holds 80%+ share (though Capvaxive is eroding at the margin), but Abrysvo faces structural disadvantage in both US older adult RSV (Arexvy leads) and infant RSV (monoclonal standard established). Penbraya has not broken through against Bexsero.


The infant RSV market is now definitively monoclonal — Q1 2026 marks the first quarter where both Beyfortus and Enflonsia are commercially available. Two monoclonal options create within-class competition that permanently marginalizes maternal vaccination. This is no longer an emerging trend — it's the new baseline.


Moderna's combination pipeline is the 2-3 year structural risk — mResvia alone is a non-factor, but RSV+flu combination vaccines could compress standalone RSV revenue for all players. Pfizer has no announced combination strategy.


GSK is the competitive benchmark across every vaccine category — in RSV (Arexvy leads older adults), MenB (Bexsero holds against Penbraya), and portfolio breadth (Shingrix provides revenue stability). Pfizer's vaccine franchise is more vulnerable than its aggregate revenue suggests.



5. Thesis Update



Thesis
Status
Key Evidence This Quarter




1. Capvaxive serotype counting erodes Prevnar 20
CONFIRMED
Pfizer earnings acknowledge US share loss; ZoomRx data shows 3/7 competitive wins for Capvaxive; serotype counting narrative documented in multiple conversations


2. Abrysvo maternal won't recover (US)
UNCHANGED
No new maternal data; international maternal contributes to Q4 growth but US trend unknown until 2025-26 season data publishes


3. Adult RSV market structurally capped
STRENGTHENED
mResvia at $8M FY is a market failure; Arexvy US down 40%; only Abrysvo growing, and that's via international + label expansion, not US market growth


4. Presumptive communication = highest ROI
CONFIRMED
ZoomRx final report v2 delivers definitive data: 100% vs. ~60% acceptance based on communication style alone. Pattern vaccine-agnostic.


5. Penbraya constrained by MenB apathy
UNCHANGED
No primary data; secondary signals (low MenB coverage ~30%) persist



Net assessment: Q1 2026 is the quarter where the Pfizer Vaccines thesis framework matures from "emerging hypotheses" to "confirmed patterns with data." Theses 1 and 4 are now confirmed with primary evidence. Thesis 3 is strengthened by competitor revenue failures. The portfolio picture is clear: Prevnar is defending against Capvaxive (and eventually VAX-31), Abrysvo is growing internationally but constrained in the US, and the cross-portfolio behavioral finding (communication style) is the most actionable insight ZoomRx has produced.

6. What to Watch Next Quarter (Q2 2026)

Pfizer Q1 2026 earnings (late April 2026) — First 2026 quarterly read on Prevnar and Abrysvo. Watch for: Prevnar US share trend, Abrysvo seasonal revenue normalization, any Penbraya launch metrics.
Merck Q1 2026 earnings — Capvaxive quarterly revenue. If Capvaxive exceeds $150M quarterly, the competitive share shift is material and accelerating.
ACIP June 2026 meeting — potential review of RSV adult recommendation. Any broadening of the 75+ threshold or adult risk-factor guidance directly impacts Abrysvo's US market ceiling.
Vaxcyte VAX-31 regulatory progress — FDA interactions, Phase 3 enrollment milestones. The timeline from Phase 3 data to filing to approval determines when Pfizer and Merck face the next-gen threat.
2025-26 RSV season wrap-up data — CDC surveillance data on RSV immunization coverage, maternal vs. nirsevimab split, and vaccine effectiveness. This data resolves Thesis 2 for the US market.
ZoomRx follow-up opportunity — Pfizer's response to the final report recommendations will determine the next study scope. The most natural follow-up is a Capvaxive competitive tracking study or a cross-portfolio communication training study.
